- Pony Express/Overland route — [2] quote: "Pony Express/Overland route"
- "Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You" — [6] quote: "Letters to Judy: What Kids Wish They Could Tell You"

Ambiguities:
- "Pony Express/Overland route" bundles two services ("Pony Express" and "Overland") — which delivered what is unclear from the passages.
- The token "Letters" appears as a book title ([6]) and could be conflated with literal mailed "letters".

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do NOT state which items (e.g., "mail", "letters", "packages") were delivered to Trout Creek, nor do they explicitly confirm that Pony Express or Overland services delivered mail/letters to Trout Creek.

Retrieval direction:
- Open the "Pony Express" page (confirm what the Pony Express delivered and its route stops).
- Open the "Overland Mail Company" or "Overland route" page (confirm mail/passenger services and stops).
- Search query: "Trout Creek, Utah Pony Express" (or page "Trout Creek, Utah" for postal history/post office).
- Search query: "Trout Creek Utah post office history" or "Trout Creek postal service" to verify delivered items locally.

Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.

input_dict.current_prompt:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.

output.query: Trout Creek Utah Pony Express letters

retrieved_doc_snippets: Trout Creek, Utah | Trout Creek is a small farming unincorporated community, in western Juab County, Utah, United States. It is located along the Pony Express/Overland route in northern Snake Valley, north of Partoun and south of Callao. It is named after the creek that flows from the west off of the Deep Creek Mountains. It has one of the most remote Mormon chapels in Utah, with a short section of paved road, the only paved road for over 50 mi .

Mammoth Creek | Mammoth Creek is a Utah creek which flows for over 20 miles through mountains and forests from Mammoth Summit (near Brian Head, Utah), through the Mammoth Valley, to its confluence with the Sevier River (near Hatch, Utah). The creek contains wild brown trout and hatchery rainbow trout.

Ibapah Peak | Ibapah Peak is a 12018 ft summit in Juab County, Utah in the United States. It is the highest point of the Deep Creek Range and is located less than 5 mi east of the Utah-Nevada border, and about 10 mi northwest of the town of Trout Creek, Utah. With a topographic prominence of 5247 ft it is the fifth-most prominent summit in Utah.
Rock Creek Station | Rock Creek Station was a stagecoach and Pony Express station in southeastern Nebraska, near the present-day village of Endicott.

Gandy, Utah | Gandy is a small farming unincorporated community in Millard County, Utah, United States, located just east of the Nevada border. It is located in the west-central part of Snake Valley. It is known for Gandy Warm Springs and Gandy Creek, a large spring (15-19 cfs) that comes out of the base of Spring Mountain to the west. It stays around 81–82 degrees Fahrenheit (27–28 Celsius) year-round. Originally known as Smithville, Gandy was renamed in 1925 after Isaac Gandy, the first ranch  ...[truncated]

National Pony Express Association | National Pony Express Association (NPEA) is a non-profit, volunteer-led historical organization. Its purpose is to preserve the original Pony Express trail and to continue the memory and importance of Pony Express in American history in partnership with the National Park Service, Pony Express Trail Association, and the Oregon-California Trails Association.

Navajo Lake (Utah) | Navajo Lake is a small lake in northwestern Kane County in southern Utah. It is shallow, reaching depths of only 25 feet. Recreation includes fishing, boating, swimming, and lodging facilities. Species of trout in Navajo Lake are brook trout, splake trout (hybrid), and rainbow trout. Trout survival for winter is very poor with only the splake and brook trout overwintering. There is also a dense population of Utah chub.
